food_name: Double Unagi and Beef Rice Bowl
aliases: Double Eel and Beef Rice Bowl

english: Double Eel and Beef Rice Bowl

food_subgroup: Mixed protein rice bowl (donburi) with grilled eel and beef

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for unagi-and-beef donburi. FOODON_03311737 (rice bowl food product) is the nearest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for unagi-beef donburi. A00LC (rice-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible parent category in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific Japanese rice bowl preparation. SNOMED CT does not provide sufficient granularity for this dish.

main_ingredients: tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a double portion of grilled unagi fillets, a serving of simmered beef with onion, and a large bed of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, as typically served at Sukiya-style Japanese fast-casual restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175135); Beef, short loin, top loin steak, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, broiled (FDC ID 174032); White rice, cooked, unenriched (FDC ID 168878); and tare sauce estimated from soy sauce and sugar components. Weighted composite for a double-unagi-beef donburi.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a double unagi and beef rice bowl. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked eel, cooked beef, cooked white rice, and soy-based tare sauce. Approximate bowl composition assumed: 200 g cooked rice, 160 g grilled eel (double portion), 120 g simmered beef with onion, 70 g tare sauce and broth. Per-100g values derived from this 550 g composite. Values should be treated as estimates pending laboratory analysis or an HPB/USDA branded entry for this specific product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice (cooked, Japanese short-grain) used as primary GI anchor; analogous to 'Steamed white rice, Japanese' reported in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ~72). Composite dish GI dominated by the large white rice component.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a double unagi and beef rice bowl. The GI of 72 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked Japanese short-grain white rice, which constitutes approximately 36% of the bowl by weight and is the dominant glycemic contributor. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 108.9) / 100 ≈ 78.4, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 19.8 × 5.5 = 108.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from tare sauce and soy-based broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish, as a single 550 g serving provides approximately 1705 mg sodium — over 70% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from the soy-based tare sauce and dashi broth used in both the unagi glaze and the beef simmering liquid.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from large white rice portion and added sugars in tare sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish, as the double-portion bowl delivers an estimated glycemic load of approximately 78 per serving driven by a large bed of high-GI Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~72) and the sugar-containing tare glaze; portion reduction or substitution with lower-GI grain alternatives is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef, partially offset by cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids from grilled eel
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the grilled eel component provides beneficial EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids that may support cardiovascular health, but the beef component contributes saturated fat and cholesterol, and the high sodium content from tare sauce poses an additional cardiovascular risk; overall consumption should be moderated and paired with sodium-reduction strategies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Double Unagi and Beef Rice Bowl (ダブルうなぎ牛丼, Daburu Unagi Gyudon) is a Japanese donburi dish combining two key protein components over a large bed of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The unagi component consists of a double portion of freshwater eel (Anguilla japonica) fillets that have been grilled over charcoal or a gas flame using the kabayaki technique: the eel is repeatedly basted with a sweet-savory tare sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar, producing a lacquered, caramelised exterior with a tender, fatty interior. The beef component is thinly sliced beef simmered in a dashi-based broth with onion and seasoning, in the style of gyudon. Together, the dish delivers a rich umami flavour profile with sweetness from the tare glaze, savoriness from the simmered beef, and the characteristic soft, slightly fatty texture of grilled eel. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with a full 550 g serving providing around 924 kcal. It is a significant source of high-quality protein (~59 g per serving), vitamin B12, vitamin D, and omega-3 fatty acids from the eel. However, the dish carries a high glycemic load (~78 per serving) due to the large white rice base, and a high sodium burden (~1705 mg per serving) from the tare sauce and broth, making it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish is served at Japanese fast-casual chains such as Sukiya, where it is a premium menu item combining two traditionally valued Japanese ingredients.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific commercial dish (Double Unagi and Beef Rice Bowl as served at Sukiya Singapore). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the individual components (cooked eel, cooked beef, white rice, tare sauce). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese short-grain white rice as the dominant glycemic contributor. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the compositional assumptions are defensible for a standard donburi of this type.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no HPB Singapore or branded laboratory analysis was available for this specific dish. Values should be updated if an official branded or laboratory-verified entry becomes available. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Sukiya double-unagi donburi portion sizes; actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. Sodium content is high due to tare sauce and dashi broth; exact sodium will vary by restaurant preparation. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not confirmed by a single-source laboratory analysis for this dish. 5. Unagi (freshwater eel) is not halal-certified in most Islamic jurisprudence contexts; is_halal is set to false. 6. GI of 72 is from the University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese short-grain white rice (analogous estimate); no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycemic load per serving = (72 × 108.9) / 100 ≈ 78.4, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 19.8 g/100g × 5.5 = 108.9 g. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as closest-parent matches; no exact ontology codes exist for this specific dish. 8. cuisine_tags from the input (Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers, Drinks & Beverages, Fried Chicken) appear to be restaurant-level tags not specific to this dish and have not been carried forward into the Veritas record.
